hypothesis: Mutating position 5 to aromatic residues (W/F/Y) will enhance complementarity with SSTR2-specific ECL2/TM5 contacts, while mutating position 11 to hydrophobic residues (L/I/V) will reduce off-target SSTR3/SSTR5 affinity by avoiding electrostatic clashes in those subtypes. This will increase the selectivity margin (Δmargin) by strengthening SSTR2 binding and weakening off-target binding.

critic_feedback_used.hypothesis: The current hypothesis is that SSTR2 selectivity can be achieved by optimizing non-pharmacophore positions to disrupt off-target interactions. This iteration supports that hypothesis, but the low pass rate suggests the need for more targeted mutagenesis and tighter constraints on selectivity-driven design.

proposed_changes.rationale: To improve selectivity, we must target non-pharmacophore positions that are known to contribute to off-target interactions (SSTR3/SSTR5). Focusing mutations at these positions will help disrupt conserved pocket contacts while preserving the pharmacophore (FWKT(7-10) and Cys3/Cys14).

These mutations are designed to disrupt conserved interactions in SSTR3/SSTR5 binding pockets without affecting the pharmacophore. For example, A1K and A2R introduce charged residues that may repel off-target interactions, while T5D and T6E may break hydrogen bonding patterns in SSTR3/SSTR5.
